questions.stem: What is the atomic number of nitrogen?
questions.explanation: The atomic number equals the number of protons; nitrogen has 7.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

hints.prompt: What does an element's atomic number equal?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Its number of protons plus neutrons

hints.rationale: The atomic number is defined as the proton count.

hints.prompt: To answer, you must determine the proton count of a nitrogen atom. That value is...

choices.label: D
choices.text: a fixed number that identifies nitrogen

hints.rationale: Every atom of an element shares the same defining proton number.

hints.prompt: Which is a trap answer to avoid here?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A value that is actually a different element's atomic number

choices.label: B
choices.text: The value equal to this element's proton count

hints.rationale: Distractors are often other elements' atomic numbers; anchor on this element's protons.

questions.stem: In a perfectly inelastic collision, which quantity is conserved?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Neither momentum nor kinetic energy is conserved.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Total momentum, while kinetic energy is not fully conserved.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Kinetic energy is conserved but momentum is not.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Both momentum and kinetic energy are conserved.

questions.explanation: Momentum is conserved in all collisions with no external force; kinetic energy is conserved only in elastic collisions.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

hints.prompt: In any collision with no net external force, which quantity is always conserved?
hints.rationale: Momentum is conserved whenever no net external force acts on the system.

hints.prompt: In which kind of collision is kinetic energy also conserved?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Any collision where the objects stick together

hints.rationale: Kinetic energy is fully conserved only in elastic collisions; inelastic ones convert some of it.

hints.prompt: For the sticking-together (perfectly inelastic) case, which idea is a common misconception to avoid?

choices.label: A
choices.text: That the objects move together afterward

choices.label: B
choices.text: That kinetic energy must be fully conserved just because momentum is

choices.label: C
choices.text: That some kinetic energy is converted to heat/deformation

hints.rationale: Momentum conservation does not force kinetic-energy conservation; inelastic collisions lose usable KE.

questions.stem: Adding another resistor in parallel causes the total resistance to
questions.explanation: Adding parallel paths gives current more routes, lowering total resistance below the smallest branch.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

hints.prompt: Adding another resistor in parallel changes the number of paths available to the current how?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It adds another path for current

choices.label: B
choices.text: It removes a path for current

choices.label: C
choices.text: It leaves the number of paths unchanged

hints.rationale: Each parallel branch gives the current an additional route.

hints.prompt: For resistors in parallel, the equivalent resistance is always...

choices.label: A
choices.text: equal to the average of the branches

choices.label: B
choices.text: less than the smallest single branch

choices.label: C
choices.text: equal to the sum of the branches

hints.rationale: A parallel combination always drops below the smallest branch resistance.

hints.prompt: Which reasoning is the trap that leads to a wrong choice here?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Recognizing more branches lower the total

choices.label: B
choices.text: Comparing the result against the smallest branch

choices.label: C
choices.text: Assuming resistance adds up (goes up) the way it does in series

choices.label: D
choices.text: Noting that extra paths make it easier for current to flow

hints.rationale: Series intuition (adding) is the trap; parallel branches lower the total resistance.

questions.stem: A sample starts with 80 g of a radioisotope whose half-life is 2 days. How much remains after 6 days?
questions.explanation: Number of half-lives = 6/2 = 3; remaining = 80 x (1/2)^3 = 10 g.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

hints.prompt: How much of a radioisotope remains after some elapsed time?
hints.rationale: Halve the amount once per half-life elapsed.

hints.prompt: Which setup correctly applies the values here?

choices.label: C
choices.text: number of half-lives = 6 / 2, then remaining = 80 x (1/2)^(that number)

hints.rationale: Find how many half-lives elapsed, then halve repeatedly.

hints.prompt: Which is a common mistake to avoid on this problem?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Writing the formula down before substituting the numbers

choices.label: C
choices.text: Checking that the final answer's units make sense

choices.label: D
choices.text: Treating decay as linear (subtracting equal amounts) instead of repeatedly halving

hints.rationale: Avoid this error; the other listed habits are actually good practice.

choices.label: C

choices.label: D

choices.label: A

choices.label: C

questions.stem: According to Dalton's law, the total pressure of a gas mixture equals

choices.label: A
choices.text: the sum of the partial pressures of the components.

choices.label: B
choices.text: the pressure of the heaviest gas only.

choices.label: C
choices.text: the average of the partial pressures.

choices.label: D
choices.text: the product of the partial pressures.

hints.prompt: What does the 'partial pressure' of one gas in a mixture mean?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The pressure that gas alone would exert if it occupied the whole container

choices.label: B
choices.text: The gas's share of the container's volume

choices.label: C
choices.text: The pressure of only the densest gas

choices.label: D
choices.text: The total pressure divided by the number of gases

hints.rationale: Partial pressure is the pressure a single component would exert by itself.

hints.prompt: Dalton's law treats the gases in an ideal mixture as...

choices.label: B
choices.text: independent contributors that do not interact

choices.label: C
choices.text: reactants that combine into one pressure

choices.label: D
choices.text: contributing only through the heaviest molecule

hints.rationale: Ideal gases in a mixture act independently of one another.

hints.prompt: If someone computed a total pressure SMALLER than one of the individual partial pressures, that result is...

choices.label: A
choices.text: correct only for the heaviest gas

choices.label: C
choices.text: impossible, since the total cannot be less than any single component

hints.rationale: The total must be at least as large as any one partial pressure, ruling out averaging or cancellation.
